text: I should have studied more for that test.
I could have taken the earlier train.
She might have missed the bus.
I would have passed if I had tried.
So, how was the test? The outcome was not great, was it?
No. Honestly, I should have started earlier. I was reckless and left it for the last night.
You could have asked me to study together. We would have covered everything in a calmer way.
True. Studying at midnight backfired -- I was too tired. I overlooked the basics completely.
Do not be too hard on yourself. It was a worthwhile lesson. Next time, be more cautious with your plan.
You are right. If I spare an hour a day, a better grade is bound to come. I will not hesitate next time.
I could have asked for help.
In the end, it was a worthwhile lesson.

Hi Mark. Last class you talked about wishes and regrets. Today we go one step further: we look back at the past and imagine a different outcome. For this, English uses modal perfects: a modal verb plus have plus the past participle. Here is the key. Use should have plus the past participle to talk about the right thing to do that you did not do, like I should have studied more. Use could have to talk about a possibility that existed but did not happen, like I could have taken the earlier train. Use might have or may have when you are not sure, when you only guess about the past, like She might have missed the bus. And use would have for the imagined result of a different choice, like I would have passed if I had tried. Notice that in fast speech, should have often sounds like should've, and would have sounds like would've. The whole idea is to look back and judge or guess what was possible. So think about yesterday. What should you have done? What could have happened if you had chosen differently? See you in class, Mark.

Let me look back at something that happened to me last week. I had a math test, and honestly, the outcome was not great. In hindsight, I should have started studying earlier instead of leaving everything for the last night. I could have asked my teacher for help, but I hesitated, and that was a reckless choice. The plan to study at midnight backfired completely, because I was too tired to focus. If I had been more cautious, I might have done much better. I keep thinking I would have passed easily if I had spared just one hour a day during the week. It was a hard lesson, but a worthwhile one. Now I know that good preparation is bound to pay off, and that pulling off a good grade at the last minute is almost never inevitable. Next time, I will not overlook the basics.

First, you decide what you want to say about the past: a criticism, a missed chance, a guess, or an imagined result. Then, for the right thing you did not do, you use should have plus the past participle. Next, for a chance that existed but did not happen, you use could have. After that, for a guess when you are not sure, you use might have or may have. Finally, for the imagined result of a different choice, you use would have, often with an if clause.
We could have won the game.
I was too reckless with my time.
If I spare an hour a day, success is bound to come.
In the end, it was a worthwhile lesson.
I could have asked for help.
She might have missed the bus.
I would have passed if I had tried.
